title: Fort Rich closes wolf range
summary: Following a series of dicey encounters between people with dogs and an emboldened pack of wolves, U.S. Army officials on Fort Richardson announced Saturday that they are placing all training areas west and north of the Artillery Road area off limits. The restriction is being imposed for the safety of people who use Fort Rich lands for recreational purposes, base officials said in a written statement. The most recent attack by a group of wolves known as the Elmendorf pack happened Thursday morning, when three women jogging down Artillery Road with their dogs were surprised to find seven or eight wolves trailing them. The pack silently approached from behind, and by the time the women noticed, the wolves spanned the road only a few feet behind them. One dog took injuries that required stitch…

title: New parks superintendent trades ATV for desk
summary: Brooks Ludwig will spend more time sitting behind a desk than a snowmachine or four-wheeler now that he's been named superintendent for the northern area of Alaska State Parks. "I'll miss being out in the field," said Ludwig, who has spent the last 4 1/2 years as chief ranger for Alaska State Parks in Fairbanks and the previous 20 years working as a seasonal and full-time ranger in Delta Junction. "As the superintendent, I still want to have that field connection and do ride-alongs with staff and keep my hands on the pulse of the operation," Ludwig said. Nonetheless, Ludwig said it was time to hang up his holster in favor of a desk job. A father of three young boys, including twin 4-year-olds, Ludwig said his new job offers more stability and family time than his ranger positions, which of…

title: Alaska skiers named to U.S. nordic team
summary: Alaska-based skiers make up a hefty chunk of the women named Thursday to the 16-member U.S. Cross Country Ski Team for the 2008 season. Two-time Olympian Kikkan Randall, 24, had the first podium finish by a U.S. woman at a World Cup race last season. She is the only woman on the five member A team. Taz Mannix of Talkeetna and her new teammate at Alaska Pacific University, Laura Valaas, are both on the B team. World Cup podium performers Andy Newell of Shaftsbury, Vt., and Torin Koos of Leavenworth, Wash., head the men's team. Not since an 18-member team was named for the 1992 Olympic season has the U.S. team been so large. "We've been predominantly a men's team for several years ... but now we're seeing that dynamic change a bit," U.S. Nordic Director Luke Bodensteiner said. "We have more …

title: Illegal hunting outfitters hit with big fine
summary: A family from Maine that wanted to run a remote lodge in Alaska will have to pay approximately $70,000 in fines and spend the next 10 years on probation after getting tangled in Alaska's web of hunting and fishing regulations. Lester and Marie Conklin of Conklins Lodge & Camps in Patten, Maine, bought a lodge on Donkey Lake near Skwentna and tried to make it go by selling fishing trips and sidling around state laws on bear hunting. State laws say only licensed guides can help out hunters in Alaska, but obtaining a license as a guide requires serving an apprenticeship as an assistant guide. To get around that requirement, the Conklins, according to court documents, sold fishermen at their lodge access to black-bear bait stations. That, according to Alaska State Troopers, was a violation of …

title: 'Sterile' lake produces huge trout
summary: A photograph of a hefty lake trout pulled from Long Lake along the Glenn Highway has sparked an investigation into whether the state has a new, resident population of the far north char. Matanuska-Susitna area resident Cindy Larson caught the 34-inch-long, 22-pound fat-bellied laker Sept. 27 on her second fishing trip to the long, thin lake that fills an old Talkeetna Mountain canyon just off the Glenn Highway not far from Chickaloon. Larson admitted to being "hugely surprised. We go there thinking we're going to be skunked; half an hour later ..." But that was only the beginning of the surprises. When Cindy's husband, Rick Larson, posted a picture of the fish in the "Nice Catch" gallery at www.adn.com , a lot of other people were surprised. Why? Long Lake isn't supposed to have lake trout…

title: Illegal big-four hunt brings $22,000 fine
summary: One Washington state man has been fined more than $22,000 and Alaska Wildlife Troopers have an arrest warrant for another man after a hunt last fall in which they illegally killed brown bear, caribou, moose and Dall sheep, troopers said Wednesday. "This is actually a very serious case," trooper investigator Robert Welch said. "It was a clear and deliberate attempt to go after multiple animals illegally." Carson Kemmer, 24, pleaded no contest Friday to two counts of taking brown bear and Dall sheep without a guide, two counts of taking big game without a tag, one count of failure to salvage the edible meat of a big game animal and one count of hunting without a license. He was originally charged with 22 hunting violations. Troopers are looking for Kemmer's hunting companion, 53-year-old Jos…

title: If alive, Fossett faces challenge tougher than Iditarod
summary: JIM LAVRAKAS / Daily News archive 1992 enlarge Millionaire adventurer Steve Fossett ran his second and final Iditarod in 1992. title: Seven-day skiing opens at Alyeska
summary: More outdoors stories » Illegal hunting outfitters hit with big fine Gas prices force snowmachiners to consider costs Seven-day skiing opens at Alyeska Arctic Valley plans to open Alpenglow run Water hazard Seven-day skiing opens at Alyeska Published: December 4, 2007 Last Modified: December 4, 2007 at 04:26 AM Seven-day operations have begun 10:30 a.m. to 4 p.m. at Alyeska Resort. Story tools Comment on this story E-mail a friend Print Digg this Seed Newsvine Send link via AIM Font size : A | A | A However, the lower mountain remains closed with limited snow cover. The tram and Chair 6 are both open. The open upper portion of the mountain is primarily intermediate and above terrain. Day lodge services are closed until Saturday.
